context:
Section 8 (Conclusions and Future Work), page 35

Second, it would be interesting to understand whether Scarf’s algorithm converges in polynomial time on the bipartite matching polytope for any ordinal matrix \(C\). This would require an understanding of the problem that goes beyond stable marriages.
title: Polynomial-time convergence of Scarf’s algorithm on the bipartite matching polytope for arbitrary ordinal matrices
latex:
Let \(k\in\mathbb{N}\) and let \(G=(M\cup W,E)\) be the complete bipartite graph with \(|M|=|W|=k\). Let \(n:=|M\cup W|=2k\) and let \(m:=|E|=k^2\). Index the edges as \(E=\{e_{n+1},\dots,e_{n+m}\}\) and introduce one loop variable \(e_i=(v_i,v_i)\) for each vertex \(v_i\in M\cup W\), for \(i\in[n]\). Let \(A\in\{0,1\}^{n\times(n+m)}\) be the node–edge incidence matrix augmented with loop columns so that for each vertex \(v_i\) and each column corresponding to an edge \(e\), \(A_{i,e}=1\) iff \(v_i\) is incident to \(e\) (including loops), and let \(b:=\mathbf{1}\in\mathbb{R}^n\). Define the (bipartite) matching polytope in standard form
\[
P:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n+m}_{\ge 0}: Ax=b\}.
\]
An integer matrix \(C\in\mathbb{Z}^{n\times(n+m)}\) is an ordinal matrix if all its entries are distinct and for every row index \(i\in[n]\), every loop column \(i\), every other loop column \(j\in[n]\setminus\{i\}\), and every non-loop column \(k\in\{n+1,\dots,n+m\}\), one has \(c_{i,i} < c_{i,k} < c_{i,j}\).

Consider Scarf’s pivoting algorithm applied to input \((A,b,C)\), i.e., the algorithm that maintains a feasible basis \(B\) of \(Ax=b\) and an ordinal basis \(D\) of \(C\) with \(|B\cap D|\ge n-1\), performs alternating cardinal and ordinal pivots as in Scarf’s algorithm, and terminates when \(B=D\).

Problem: Determine whether, for every \(k\in\mathbb{N}\) and every ordinal matrix \(C\) as above, Scarf’s algorithm on input \((A,b,C)\) converges to a dominating basis in a number of pivots bounded by a polynomial in \(n\) (equivalently, polynomial in \(k\)), i.e., whether there exists a polynomial \(p(\cdot)\) such that the algorithm terminates within at most \(p(n)\) iterations for all such inputs.
open_problem_quote: Second, it would be interesting to understand whether Scarf’s algorithm converges in polynomial time on the bipartite matching polytope for any ordinal matrix \(C\). This would require an understanding of the problem that goes beyond stable marriages.

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
